item.title: New Edits
item.content:encoded:
In its prior state, this was worthy of a no-vote (I agreed with what was said above.) In this state, Its not my favourite SCP ever made, but it's definitely worthy of an upvote. Its imagery, which I feel was the strongest part of the old article, is much more vivid, and the story builds up to a point rather than having two different 'competing' threads.

+ 1

item.title: alright till the test logs.
item.content:encoded: Interesting scp, fairly well written, then the test logs just take it in the opposite direction as the rest of the scp. -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you both very much for the detailed feedback! I’ll think about ways to rework based on these comments.

item.title: testing logs
item.content:encoded:
I like the testing logs. Before I read them I was beginning to think this SCP was to helpful with no downsides. The testing logs were a good demonstration of why a debriefing was required for the use of this scp to be ethical.
The logs could be improved if the D-class were moved to different rooms during the tests. Each room could have an air leak coming from a different location. What did the foundation learn by keeping d-class in the same room with the leak coming from under the door? Right now the logs suggest the Foundation was being cruel for no reason. If the D-class were moved into different nearly air tight rooms, it would show the foundation was using the tests to fix it's airtight rooms. They just didn't think it was necessary to tell the D-class that the blood was representative of air flow. Then they would learn that keeping the d-class in the dark (pun intended) was harmful and change the containment procedures.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have to agree with Roget, both in terms of the clinical tone and the ending. I'm not as enthralled with the blood perception effect as Roget, but I do agree that it could be put to good effect with the Foundation utility thing. I'm not entirely sure a testing log is what this skip needs, but maybe you can make the utilization of the anomaly by the Foundation less overt and more gradual.

The description right now is, besides being at times clinical, describing things that don't really need to be described in a Description, and would be better served with a more imagery-focused addendum. I'm thinking a gas leak occurs, and the only person who detects the non-air-tight sealing is some random janitor who was exposed to the anomaly, and that's how the Foundation uses it. As of now, the description is a classic case of telling instead of showing, which can be fixed with a reordering and refocusing of events.

My suggestions are thus:

  • Remove unnecessary sentences: This is part of making this document more clinical. By getting rid of unnecessary sentences, then you can get to the good stuff faster. Sentences like "Its physical qualities conform to that of a standard consumer-grade video tape, but it does not show signs of wear or deterioration that typically occurs with those products." are unnecessary, especially, since we have an image. We don't need the text to tell us its a standard video tape, we can see it. Don't tell us what makes it normal, tell us what makes it unique.
  • Remove the testing logs: The testing logs try to turn this into a horror-piece when it doesn't need to be. Plus, it doesn't really make sense. How does the D-class die in the invisible blood yet, when people are sleeping in bed for hours, they're just fine? Right now, the tesing log is serving no favors. EDIT: I think I understand why it affected the D-class and not the personnel now, but my point still stands that it distracts from the more memorable element of the skip.
  • Introduce the Foundation's utilization of the anomaly later: Right now, it's just sort of summarized in a quick addendum, and it's in the middle of the piece, but I'd rather something like that be the finisher. The Foundation using a blood tape to fix air-leaks is a lot more memorable than "D-class dies in testing log". As I said above, make it an incident log that introduces a scenario where the blood tape would be necessary, and give a short finisher of how the Foundation uses the blood tape beyond that.

This is somewhat close to being good, but as of now, it's a -1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Agreeing the tone could use some work. I think the idea of the Foundation finding an anomaly with some practical purpose and trying to put it to use would work, but the paint-by-numbers approach here isn't doing it for me. In particular, the test logs escalation of "it gets spooky and then someone dies" is a major letdown from what is a unique and interesting object effect. -1, I think that if you were to rework the testing logs to take a different direction, like perhaps other things besides non-airtight rooms being affected, that might escalate things in a way that would be more fun to read about.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it. I think the clinical tone is a bit off in some places and the wording could use some work, but the concept is there and I found it an entertaining read. +1.

item.title: Author post
item.content:encoded:
Knowledge is power, and sharing that knowledge is caring!

3/31/2020: Continuing my bad habit of coldposting every once in a while. I hope you enjoy.

EDIT 1/1/2021: I have extensively re-worked this article in light of the insightful comments that some readers generously made long ago. I hope it's enjoyed. I'm thankful to HarryBlank for making SCP-5866 and figuring out a very useful way to format mid-article edits. This made it much easier for me to apply something like that here.

Random author comments
- Access granted! Thanks for reading!

By total coincidence, I went to search the surname "Yin" to be sure it was suitable for this skip and found that its meaning includes "flourishing, blood red."

Also realized I keep using "Site 54" because it wasn't in use and I didn't want to step on anyone's toes, so taking the 5044 slot felt appropriate. Edit: I don't know how I came to think this when Site 54 is one of the few numbers listed on the list of sites. Oops. Changing it in the skip.

This is definitely grounded in of one of those thoughts that you have while trying to fall asleep at night. It's an effort to combine a few things that scare me — particularly, something that is both terrible and easily avoidable. As a kid, I was seriously creeped out by the warning sticker on the side of large buckets showing a toddler drowning in just a few inches of water. The thought of dying because of such a small margin was always chilling. I hope some of that sensation comes across here (even though, as I write this, it's now painfully clear that the toddler-killing bucket is scarier than anything I can dream up).

Thanks to djkaktus for authoring SCP-3693 and giving me a better sense of how to describe seeing with your eyes closed.

Image credit: DRs Kulturarvsprojekt
License: BY-SA 2.0
